_meta.method: Merged from two research passes: (A) Rinzai-Ōbaku head-temple directory + Sōtōshū sōdō (training monastery) list — head temples and major training monasteries; (B) Sōtōshū's official 'temples welcoming foreign practitioners' directory (sotozen-navi.com) — parish temples that host foreign zazen practitioners.
_meta.coverageEstimate: Honzan saturation for Rinzai (all 14 head temples + Manpuku-ji), broad coverage of Sōtō senmon-sōdō, plus a representative slice of Sōtōshū's foreign-friendly parish directory (page 1 of 12).
knownGaps: Sōtōshū foreign-friendly directory pages 2-12 (~209 more parish temples)
Many Rinzai sub-honzan and bekkaku jiin
Korean Seon, Vietnamese Thiền, and Chan/Zen hybrid centers inside Japan are not yet surveyed

places.name: Myōshin-ji
places.address: 64 Hanazono Myoshinji-cho, Ukyo-ku, Kyoto-shi, Kyoto 616-8035, Japan
places.notes: Head temple of the Myōshin-ji branch of the Rinzai school, established in 1337 by Emperor Hanazono with Kanzan Egen as founding abbot.

places.name: Nanzen-ji
places.address: Nanzenji Fukuchicho, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto-shi, Kyoto 606-8435, Japan
places.notes: Historic head temple of the Nanzen-ji branch of the Rinzai sect, founded in 1291 at the base of Kyoto's Higashiyama hills.

places.name: Kenchō-ji
places.notes: Head temple of the Kenchō-ji branch of Rinzai Zen and first of the Kamakura Gozan, founded in 1253 by Rankei Dōryū under Hōjō Tokiyori's patronage.

places.name: Engaku-ji
places.notes: Head temple of the Engaku-ji branch, founded in 1282 by Hōjō Tokimune and Chinese master Wuxue Zuyuan; a major Kamakura Gozan monastery with active zazen training.

places.name: Tōfuku-ji
places.notes: Headquarters of the Tōfuku-ji branch of Rinzai Zen in southeastern Kyoto, renowned for its large monastic compound and autumn maple valley used for zazen retreats.

places.name: Tenryū-ji
places.address: 68 Saga Tenryuji Susukinobabacho, Ukyo-ku, Kyoto-shi, Kyoto 616-8385, Japan
places.notes: Head temple of the Tenryū-ji branch, founded in 1339 by Ashikaga Takauji and Musō Soseki; UNESCO World Heritage site with regular Zen meditation programs.

places.name: Shōkoku-ji
places.address: Imadegawa-dori, Karasuma Higashi-iru, Kamigyo-ku, Kyoto 602-0898, Japan
places.notes: Third of the Kyoto Five Mountain temples and head of the Shōkoku-ji branch, established in 1382 by Ashikaga Yoshimitsu; maintains zazen and monastic training halls.

places.name: Eigen-ji
places.notes: Head temple of the Eigen-ji branch, founded in 1361 by Sasaki Ujiyori with Jakushitsu Genkō as founding abbot; still operates a sōdō (training monastery) for monks.

places.name: Hōkō-ji
places.address: 1577-1 Okuyama, Inasa-cho, Hamana-ku, Hamamatsu-shi, Shizuoka 431-2224, Japan
places.notes: Mountain monastery north of Hamamatsu and head of the Hōkō-ji branch, founded in 1371 by Mumon Gensen; maintains a large rural training complex for Rinzai monks.

places.name: Kōgaku-ji
places.notes: Head temple of the Kōgaku-ji branch, founded in 1380 for Bassui Tokushō; a mountain Rinzai monastery with limited public access but active monastic training.

places.name: Buttsū-ji
places.notes: Head temple of the Buttsū-ji branch in rural Hiroshima, established in the late 14th century; today a large forested Rinzai training monastery open for zazen visits.

places.name: Kokutai-ji
places.notes: Head temple of the Kokutai-ji branch in Toyama, housing one of the largest Zen monasteries in the Hokuriku region; active Rinzai training center.

places.name: Kennin-ji
places.notes: Founded in 1202 by Eisai, Kennin-ji is Kyoto's oldest Zen temple and head of the Kennin-ji branch, with active zazen and lay-oriented meditation programs.

places.name: Manpuku-ji (Ōbaku-san Manpuku-ji)
places.notes: Head temple of the Ōbaku school of Zen, founded in 1661 by Chinese master Ingen; its Chinese-style monastic compound in Uji remains an active zazen training site.

places.name: Ryūtaku-ji
places.notes: Rinzai monastery of the Myōshin-ji branch in Mishima, long associated with Hakuun Yasutani and western students; retains an active rural training sōdō.

places.name: Kokeizan Eihō-ji
places.notes: Historic Rinzai temple in Tajimi known for early-morning zazen and sutra-copying; cited as a major training monastery in the contemporary Rinzai network.

places.name: Daihonzan Eihei-ji Betsuin Chōkoku-ji (Hase-dera)
places.notes: Tokyo branch temple of Daihonzan Eihei-ji, recognized by Sōtōshū as a specialized training monastery (senmon sōdō) for monks in Nishi-Azabu.

places.name: Daihonzan Sōji-ji Sōin
places.notes: Former head temple of Sōtō Zen founded by Keizan Jōkin in 1321; now the Sōji-ji Sōin training monastery and major pilgrimage site on the Noto Peninsula.

places.name: Daiyūzan Saijō-ji
places.notes: Large mountain Sōtō monastery near Hakone, one of the sect's three great prayer temples and an officially designated specialized training sōdō.

places.name: Kasuisai (Banshōzan Kasuisai)
places.notes: Historic Sōtō temple and fire-ritual center (Akiba Sōhonden) in Fukuroi, operating a recognized training sōdō and lay zazen/temple-stay programs.

places.name: Kakuōzan Nittai-ji
places.notes: Inter-sectarian temple housing Śākyamuni relics in Nagoya; Sōtōshū designates a specialized training sōdō within Nittai-ji for Sōtō monks.

places.name: Aichi Senmon Nisōdō (Shōbō-ji)
places.lineage: Sōtō (Aichi Senmon Nisōdō, nuns' training sōdō)
places.notes: Main Sōtō training monastery for nuns in Japan, located at Shōbō-ji in Nagoya; led for decades by Aoyama Shundō Rōshi.

places.name: Tōshō-ji (Dōmatsu-ji senmon sōdō)
places.notes: Rural Sōtō monastery in Yakage with an officially recognized training sōdō; noted for traditional zen dō (meditation hall) architecture.

places.name: Zuio-ji (Bukkokusan Zuio-ji)
places.notes: Major Sōtō training monastery in Shikoku, known for its large ginkgo tree and status as the only specialized sōdō in the Shikoku region.

places.name: Ankoku-ji (Daiko-zan Ankoku-ji)
places.notes: Urban Sōtō training monastery in central Fukuoka (Tenjin), balancing rigorous monastic training with lay zazen offerings in a downtown setting.

places.name: Kōtai-ji (Kōtai-ji Senmon Sōdō)
places.notes: Historic Sōtō temple in Nagasaki's Teramachi, designated by Sōtōshū as a specialized training sōdō and known as one of the city's three great temples.

places.name: Gotanjo-ji
places.notes: Modern Sōtō monastery on the birthplace site of Keizan Jōkin, functioning since 2009 as a specialized training sōdō and popularly known as the 'cat temple.'

places.name: Shōbō-ji (Ōshū Shōbō-ji)
places.address: 129 Shōbōji, Kuroishi-cho, Mizusawa-ku, Ōshū-shi, Iwate 023-0101, Japan
places.notes: Historic Sōtō monastery in southern Iwate, once counted with Eihei-ji and Sōji-ji as a leading northern Sōtō center; maintains a large thatched-hall training complex.

places.name: Zenpō-ji (Ryūtaku-zan Zenpō-ji)
places.notes: Major Sōtō pilgrimage temple near Sakata, famed for dragon-king rites and designated as a specialized training monastery attracting monk-trainees and lay pilgrims.

places.name: Chūō-ji (Jissō-zan Chūō-ji)
places.address: 2-1 Minami 6-jō Nishi, Chūō-ku, Sapporo-shi, Hokkaidō 064-0806, Japan
places.notes: Central Sapporo Sōtō temple established 1874, hosting a specialized training sōdō and regular public zazen sessions near Susukino entertainment district.

places.name: Jōkō-ji (Jōkō-ji Senmon Sōdō)
places.notes: Sōtō temple in Kushiro's historic port district, designated as a specialized training sōdō and noted for educating clergy serving Hokkaidō parishes.

places.name: Hosshin-ji (Reishō-zan Hosshin-ji)
places.notes: Sōtō training temple in Obama known for rigorous winter takuhatsu; Harada Sekkei Rōshi served as abbot, leading intensive zazen practice for Japanese and foreign monks.

places.name: Daijō-ji (Daijō-ji Zen Monastery, Kanazawa)
places.notes: Mountain Sōtō monastery on Mt. Nodayama above Kanazawa, offering daily early-morning zazen and overnight training programs for lay visitors and overseas practitioners.

places.name: Shōgō-ji (Hōzan Dōjō Shōgō-ji)
places.notes: Remote Sōtō monastery in the mountains of Kumamoto operating the Hōzan Dōjō and long-running international ango (practice period) for foreign and Japanese practitioners.

places.name: Hōfuku-ji
places.notes: Founded 1242; head temple of the Hōfuku-ji branch of Rinzai. Sesshū studied here in his youth.

places.name: Bukkoku-ji
places.lineage: Sōtō (small training temple — Tangen Roshi line)
places.notes: Small intensive Sōtō training temple founded by Harada Tangen Roshi; long-standing destination for Western Zen practitioners.

places.name: Shōfuku-ji
places.notes: Rinzai senmon-sōdō led for many years by Yamada Mumon Rōshi; major training destination in Kobe.

places.name: Tōkō-ji
places.notes: Listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of temples that welcome foreign zazen practitioners (外国人参禅).

places.name: Unsei-ji
places.notes: Gesshōzan Unsei-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Zenshō-ji Nakatsugawa
places.notes: Listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Dairyū-ji Oga
places.notes: Kaizōzan Dairyū-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Toyokawa-ji Hiratsuka
places.notes: Hiratsukazan Toyokawa-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Anraku-ji Yamanashi
places.notes: Hōfuzan Anraku-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Fusai-ji Murakami
places.notes: Taiyōzan Fusai-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Kentoku-ji Nakagawa
places.notes: Ryūtakuzan Kentoku-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Jūrin-ji Fujinomiya
places.notes: Fujisan Jūrin-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Daiun-ji Sakuragawa
places.notes: Tomiyasan Daiun-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Chōsen-ji Nakagawa
places.notes: Shirakusan Chōsen-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Chūō-in Tomakomai
places.notes: Jitsuhōzan Chūō-in; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Jōjō-ji Izumo
places.notes: Tentokuzan Jōjō-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Eirin-ji Ogi
places.notes: Listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Shōkaku-ji Hannō
places.notes: Fukuōzan Shōkaku-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Taihei-ji Nagoya
places.notes: Listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.

places.name: Dairyū-ji Shinjuku
places.notes: Kiunzan Dairyū-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples (central Tokyo).

places.name: Zenfuku-ji Harima
places.notes: Ōsawa Banshōzan Zenfuku-ji; listed in Sōtōshū's official directory of foreign-zazen-friendly temples.
